From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Third-level administrative division of Italy

"Comunes" redirects here. For the non-profit collective, see [Comunes Collective](/wiki/Comunes_Collective "Comunes Collective"). For other uses, see [Commune (disambiguation)](/wiki/Commune_(disambiguation) "Commune (disambiguation)").

| Municipalities of Italy *Comuni* ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")) | |
| --- | --- |
| Category | [Regionalised unitary state](/wiki/Regional_state "Regional state") |
| Location | [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italy "Italy") |

| Areas | 0.1124 km2 (0.0434 sq mi) ([Atrani](/wiki/Atrani "Atrani")) – 1,286.7305 km2 (496.8094 sq mi) ([Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome")) |

| Subdivisions | * *[Frazioni](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione")*, *[circoscrizioni](/wiki/Circoscrizione "Circoscrizione")* and *[municipi](/wiki/Municipio "Municipio")* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Italy_-_Regions%2C_provinces_and_municipalities.svg/500px-Italy_-_Regions%2C_provinces_and_municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_-_Regions,_provinces_and_municipalities.svg)

**[Administrative divisions of Italy](/wiki/Italy#Administrative_divisions "Italy")**:  
[Regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") (black borders)  
[Provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy") (dark gray borders)  
*Comuni* (light grey borders)

A ***comune*** (pronounced [[koˈmuːne]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); pl.: *comuni*, pronounced [[koˈmuːni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is an [administrative division](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), roughly equivalent to a [township](/wiki/Township "Township") or [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is the third-level administrative division of Italy, after regions (*[regioni](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy")*) and provinces (*[province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy")*). The *comune* can also have the [title of](/wiki/City_status_in_Italy "City status in Italy") *città* (lit. 'city').[[2]](#cite_note-camera-2)

Formed *[praeter legem](/wiki/Praeter_legem "Praeter legem")* according to the principles consolidated in [medieval municipalities](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) the *comune* is provided for by article 114 of the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It can be divided into *[frazioni](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione")*, which in turn may have limited power due to special elective assemblies.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In the autonomous region of the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley"), a *comune* is officially called a *commune* in French.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The *comune* provides essential public services: [registry](/wiki/Civil_registry "Civil registry") of births and deaths, [registry of deeds](/wiki/Registry_of_deeds "Registry of deeds"), and maintenance of local roads and public works.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Many *comuni* have a *[Polizia Comunale](/wiki/Municipal_police_(Italy) "Municipal police (Italy)")* (lit. 'Communal Police'), which is responsible for public order duties.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The *comune* also deal with the definition and compliance with the *piano regolatore generale* (lit. 'general regulator plan'), a document that regulates the building activity within the communal area.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

All communal structures or schools, sports and cultural structures such as communal libraries, theaters, etc. are managed by the *comuni*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) *Comuni* must have their own communal statute and have a climatic and seismic classification of their territory for the purposes of hazard mitigation and [civil protection](/wiki/Civil_protection "Civil protection").[[12]](#cite_note-12) *Comuni* also deal with the [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

It is headed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor#Italy "Mayor") (*sindaco* or *sindaca*) assisted by a legislative body, the *consiglio comunale* (lit. 'communal council'), and an executive body, the *giunta comunale* (lit. 'communal committee').[[14]](#cite_note-14) The mayor and members of the *consiglio comunale* are elected together by resident citizens: the coalition of the elected mayor (who needs a relative majority or an absolute majority in the first or second round of voting, depending on the population) gains three fifths of the *consiglio*'s seats.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The *giunta comunale* is chaired by the mayor, who appoints others members, called *[assessori](/wiki/Assessor_(Italy) "Assessor (Italy)")*, one of whom serves as deputy mayor (*vicesindaco*).[[16]](#cite_note-16) The offices of the *comune* are housed in a building usually called the *municipio*, or *palazzo comunale* (lit. '[town hall](/wiki/Town_hall "Town hall")').[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As of January 2021, there were 7,904 *comuni* in Italy;[[18]](#cite_note-comuni-18) they vary considerably in size and population. For example, the *comune* of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), in [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio"), has an area of 1,287.36 km2 (497.05 sq mi) and a population of 2,758,454 inhabitants, and is both the largest and the most populated.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Mural_Crown_of_Italian_Comune_%28New_Variant%29.svg/250px-Mural_Crown_of_Italian_Comune_%28New_Variant%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mural_Crown_of_Italian_Comune_(New_Variant).svg)

[Mural crown](/wiki/Mural_crown "Mural crown") for the title of *comune*. It is located in the upper part of the coat of arms of the *comune*.

[Atrani](/wiki/Atrani "Atrani") in the [province of Salerno](/wiki/Province_of_Salerno "Province of Salerno") ([Campania](/wiki/Campania "Campania")) is the smallest *comune* by area, with only 0.1206 km2 (0.0466 sq mi),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and [Morterone](/wiki/Morterone "Morterone") ([Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy")) is the smallest by population.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Many present-day *comuni* trace their roots along timescales spanning centuries and at times millennia.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The northernmost *comune* is [Predoi](/wiki/Predoi "Predoi"), the southernmost one [Lampedusa e Linosa](/wiki/Lampedusa_e_Linosa "Lampedusa e Linosa"), the westernmost [Bardonecchia](/wiki/Bardonecchia "Bardonecchia") and the easternmost [Otranto](/wiki/Otranto "Otranto").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The *comune* with the longest name is [San Valentino in Abruzzo Citeriore](/wiki/San_Valentino_in_Abruzzo_Citeriore "San Valentino in Abruzzo Citeriore"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) while the *comuni* with the shortest name are [Lu](/wiki/Lu,_Piedmont "Lu, Piedmont"), [Ro](/wiki/Ro,_Emilia-Romagna "Ro, Emilia-Romagna"), [Ne](/wiki/Ne,_Liguria "Ne, Liguria"), [Re](/wiki/Re,_Piedmont "Re, Piedmont") and [Vo'](/wiki/Vo%27 "Vo'").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The population density of the *comuni* varies widely by [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy") and [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy"). The [province of Barletta-Andria-Trani](/wiki/Province_of_Barletta-Andria-Trani "Province of Barletta-Andria-Trani"), for example, has 381,091 inhabitants in 10 *comuni*,[[27]](#cite_note-27) or over 39,000 inhabitants per *comune*; whereas the [province of Isernia](/wiki/Province_of_Isernia "Province of Isernia") has 81,415 inhabitants in 52 *comuni*,[[28]](#cite_note-28) or 1,640 inhabitants per *comune*—roughly 24 times more communal units per inhabitant.

The coats of arms of the *comuni* are assigned by decree of the [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") by the Office of State Ceremonial and Honors, Honors and Heraldry Service (division of the Presidency of the Council born from the transformation of the Royal *[Consulta Araldica](/wiki/Consulta_Araldica "Consulta Araldica")*, eliminated pursuant to the provisions final of the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy")).[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Administrative subdivisions within *comuni* vary according to their population size.

*Comuni* with at least 250,000 residents are divided into *[circoscrizioni](/wiki/Circoscrizione "Circoscrizione")*[[31]](#cite_note-31) (roughly equivalent to French [arrondissements](/wiki/Municipal_arrondissements_of_France "Municipal arrondissements of France") or [London boroughs](/wiki/London_borough "London borough")) to which the *comune* delegates administrative functions such as the running of schools, social services and waste collection; the delegated functions vary from *comune* to *comune*. These bodies are headed by an elected president and a local council.

Smaller *comuni* usually comprise:

* A main city, town or village, that almost always gives its name to the *comune*; such a place is referred to as the *capoluogo* (lit. 'head-place' or 'capital'; [cf.](/wiki/Confer "Confer")Tooltip confer the [French](/wiki/France "France") *[chef-lieu](/wiki/Chef-lieu#In_France "Chef-lieu")*) of the *comune*; the word *comune* is also used in casual speech to refer to the city hall.
* Outlying areas often called *[frazioni](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione")* (sg.: *frazione*, abbreviated: *fraz.*; lit. 'fraction'), each usually centred on a small town or village. These *frazioni* usually never had pasts as independent settlements, but occasionally are former smaller *comuni* consolidated into a larger one. They may also represent settlements which predate the *capoluogo*. The ancient town of [Pollentia](/wiki/Pollentia "Pollentia") (today Pollenzo), for instance, is a *[frazione](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione")* of [Bra](/wiki/Bra_(CN) "Bra (CN)"). In recent years the *frazioni* have become more important due to the institution of the *consiglio di frazione* (lit. 'fraction council'), a local form of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") which can interact with the *comune* to address local needs, requests and claims. Even smaller places are called *[località](/wiki/Localit%C3%A0 "Località")* (abbreviated: *loc.*; lit. 'localities').
* Smaller administrative divisions called *[municipi](/wiki/Municipio "Municipio")*,[[32]](#cite_note-32) which are similar to [districts](/wiki/District "District") and [neighbourhoods](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood").

Sometimes a *frazione* might be more populated than the *capoluogo*; and rarely, owing to unusual circumstances (such as depopulation), the town hall and its administrative functions can be moved to one of the *frazioni*, but the *comune* still retains the name of the *capoluogo*.

In some cases, a *comune* might not have the same name as the *capoluogo*. In these cases, it is a *comune sparso* (lit. '[dispersed](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sparse "wikt:sparse") municipality') and the *frazione* which hosts the town hall (*municipio*) is a *sede municipale* (compare [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat")).

### *Rione*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Rione")]

See also: [Rioni of Rome](/wiki/Rioni_of_Rome "Rioni of Rome")

Some towns refer to neighborhoods within a *comune* as a ***rione*** (Italian: [[riˈoːne]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); pl.: *rioni*) or a *contrada* (pl.: *contrade*). The term originated from the administrative divisions of Rome, and is derived from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *regio* (pl.: *regiones*), 'region'. All currently extant *rioni* are located in [Municipio I](/wiki/Municipio_I "Municipio I") of Rome.[[33]](#cite_note-treccani-33) The term has been adopted as a synonym of *[quartiere](/wiki/Quartiere "Quartiere")* in the Italian *comuni*.[[33]](#cite_note-treccani-33) *Terzieri*, *quartieri*, *sestieri*, *rioni*, and their analogues are usually no longer administrative divisions of these towns, but historical and traditional communities, seen especially in towns' annual [Palio](/wiki/Palio "Palio").

#### *Terziere*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Terziere")]

A ***terziere*** (pl.: *terzieri*) is a subdivision of several towns in Italy. The word derives from *[terzo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/terzo#Italian "wikt:terzo")* (lit. 'third') and is thus used only for towns divided into three neighborhoods. *Terzieri* are most commonly found in [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria"), for example in [Trevi](/wiki/Trevi,_Umbria "Trevi, Umbria"), [Spello](/wiki/Spello "Spello"), [Narni](/wiki/Narni "Narni") and [Città della Pieve](/wiki/Citt%C3%A0_della_Pieve "Città della Pieve"); towns divided into *terzieri* in other regions include [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca") in [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), and [Ancona](/wiki/Ancona "Ancona") and [Macerata](/wiki/Macerata "Macerata") in the [Marches](/wiki/March_(territory) "March (territory)"). The medieval [Lordship of Negroponte](/wiki/Triarchy_of_Negroponte "Triarchy of Negroponte"), on the island of [Euboea](/wiki/Euboea "Euboea"), was also divided into three distinct rulerships, which were known as *terzieri*.

#### *Quartiere*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Quartiere")]

A ***quartiere*** (Italian: [[kwarˈtjɛːre]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); pl.: *quartieri*) is a territorial subdivision, properly used, for towns divided into four neighborhoods (*quarto*; lit. 'fourth') by the two main roads. It has been later used as a synonymous of neighbourhood, and an Italian town can be now subdivided into a larger number of *quartieri*. The Swiss town of [Lugano](/wiki/Lugano "Lugano") (in the Italian-speaking canton of Ticino) is also subdivided into quarters.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The English word *[quarter](/wiki/Quarter_(urban_subdivision) "Quarter (urban subdivision)")* to mean an urban neighbourhood (e.g. the French Quarter in New Orleans, Louisiana) is derived from the cognate old French word *quartier*.

#### *Sestiere*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sestiere")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Sestieri_di_Venezia.svg/250px-Sestieri_di_Venezia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sestieri_di_Venezia.svg)

Castello

San Marco

Dorsoduro

San Polo

Santa Croce

A ***sestiere*** (pl.: *sestieri*) is a subdivision of certain Italian towns and cities. The word is from *sesto* (lit. 'sixth'), so it is thus used only for towns divided into six districts. The best-known example is the [*sestieri* of Venice](/wiki/Venice#Sestieri "Venice"), but [Ascoli Piceno](/wiki/Ascoli_Piceno "Ascoli Piceno"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Rapallo](/wiki/Rapallo "Rapallo"), for example, were also divided into *sestieri*. The medieval [Lordship of Negroponte](/wiki/Triarchy_of_Negroponte "Triarchy of Negroponte"), on the island of [Euboea](/wiki/Euboea "Euboea"), was also at times divided into six districts, each with a separate ruler, through the arbitration of Venice, which were known as *sestieri*. The island of [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), a Venetian colony (the [Kingdom of Candia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Candia "Kingdom of Candia")) from the [Fourth Crusade](/wiki/Fourth_Crusade "Fourth Crusade"), was also divided into six parts, named after the *sestieri* of Venice herself, while the capital [Candia](/wiki/Heraklion "Heraklion") retained the status of a *comune* of Venice. The island of [Burano](/wiki/Burano "Burano") north of Venice is also subdivided into *sestieri*.

A variation of the word is occasionally found: the *comune* of [Leonessa](/wiki/Leonessa "Leonessa"), for example, is divided into *sesti* or sixths.

## Homonymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Homonymy")]

There are not many perfect homonymous *comuni*. There are only five cases in 10 *comuni*:[[35]](#cite_note-35)

* Castro: [Castro, Apulia](/wiki/Castro,_Apulia "Castro, Apulia") and [Castro, Lombardy](/wiki/Castro,_Lombardy "Castro, Lombardy")
* Livo: [Livo, Lombardy](/wiki/Livo,_Lombardy "Livo, Lombardy") and [Livo, Trentino](/wiki/Livo,_Trentino "Livo, Trentino")
* Peglio: [Peglio, Lombardy](/wiki/Peglio,_Lombardy "Peglio, Lombardy") and [Peglio, Marche](/wiki/Peglio,_Marche "Peglio, Marche")
* Samone: [Samone, Piedmont](/wiki/Samone,_Piedmont "Samone, Piedmont") and [Samone, Trentino](/wiki/Samone,_Trentino "Samone, Trentino")
* San Teodoro: [San Teodoro, Sardinia](/wiki/San_Teodoro,_Sardinia "San Teodoro, Sardinia") and [San Teodoro, Sicily](/wiki/San_Teodoro,_Sicily "San Teodoro, Sicily")

This is mostly due to the fact the name of the province or region was appended to the name of the *comune* in order to avoid the confusion. Two provincial capitals share the name *Reggio*: [Reggio nell'Emilia](/wiki/Reggio_Emilia "Reggio Emilia"), the capital of the [province of Reggio Emilia](/wiki/Province_of_Reggio_Emilia "Province of Reggio Emilia"), in the [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") region, and [Reggio di Calabria](/wiki/Reggio_Calabria "Reggio Calabria"), the capital of the [homonymous metropolitan city](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Reggio_Calabria "Metropolitan City of Reggio Calabria"), in the [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") region. Many other towns or villages are likewise partial homonyms (e.g. [Anzola dell'Emilia](/wiki/Anzola_dell%27Emilia "Anzola dell'Emilia") and [Anzola d'Ossola](/wiki/Anzola_d%27Ossola "Anzola d'Ossola"), or [Bagnara Calabra](/wiki/Bagnara_Calabra "Bagnara Calabra") and [Bagnara di Romagna](/wiki/Bagnara_di_Romagna "Bagnara di Romagna")).

## Title of city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Comune&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Title of city")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Mural_Crown_of_Italian_City.svg/250px-Mural_Crown_of_Italian_City.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mural_Crown_of_Italian_City.svg)

Mural crown for the title of *città* ('[city](/wiki/City "City")')

The title of *città* (lit. '[city](/wiki/City "City")') in Italy is granted to *comuni* that have been awarded it by decree of the [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy") (until 1946) or of the provisional head of state (from 1946 to 1948) or, subsequently, of the [President of the Republic](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") (after 1948), on the proposal of the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Italy) "Ministry of the Interior (Italy)"), to which the *comune* concerned sends an application for a concession, by virtue of their historical, artistic, civic or demographic importance.[[2]](#cite_note-camera-2)

The *comuni* endowed with the title of *città* usually carry the golden crown above their coat of arms, except with different provisions in the decree approving the coat of arms or in the presence). "The crown of the city ([...]) is formed by a golden circle opened by eight city gates (five visible) with two cordoned walls on the margins, supporting eight towers (five visible) joined by curtain walls, all in gold and black walled."[[36]](#cite_note-36)
